title: Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa: The Living Science of Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Te Pūtaiao Ora o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku (the stream that flows through our local area), learning how our tūpuna monitored water quality through observation of native species, particularly kōura (freshwater crayfish) and kākahi (freshwater mussels). This connects to the pūrākau (legend) of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden, whose tears are said to feed the stream, teaching us about water cycles and conservation.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu indicators of stream health observed in Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Collect and analyze water quality data using both scientific instruments (pH, nitrate tests) and mātauranga Māori observations (kōura behavior, presence of riparian plants)
Compare and contrast Western scientific and mātauranga Māori approaches to monitoring freshwater ecosystems, citing specific examples from our local stream
Propose a collaborative action plan to address one identified issue in Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, integrating both scientific and cultural knowledge

activities.title: Whakatau & Whakapapa o Te Awa (Welcome and Genealogy of the Stream)
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge Hinepūkohurangi and the mauri of the water. Students listen to a local kaumātua share the pūrākau of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku and learn the traditional names and significance of key native species (kōura, kākahi, inanga) as indicators of stream health.

activities.title: Nga Tohu Ora: Cultural and Scientific Indicators
activities.description: At the stream, students work in small groups to conduct a 'Stream Health Check'. Using provided kits, they measure pH, nitrates, and turbidity. Simultaneously, they use a cultural observation guide to record the presence/absence of kōura, the clarity of the water (as Hinepūkohurangi's tears), and the health of native riparian plants like harakeke.

activities.title: Te Hōpara Pūtaiao: Data Analysis Correlations
activities.description: Back in the lab, groups create a shared data table combining their scientific measurements and cultural observations. They analyze the data for correlations, e.g., 'When nitrate levels are high, what happens to the number of kōura we observed?' They discuss why these correlations might exist.

activities.title: He Kōrerorero: A Conversation of Knowledges
activities.description: Students participate in a guided forum (fishbowl style) debating the statement: 'The most accurate understanding of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku's health comes from weaving together data from the pH meter and the stories of the kōura.' They must use evidence from their fieldwork to support their claims.

activities.title: Te Mahere Herenga Wai: Creating an Integrated Action Plan
activities.description: Based on their findings, each group designs a one-page action plan (infographic or digital poster) to address a key issue for the stream (e.g., planting natives to reduce nitrates, building a kōura habitat). The plan must propose specific actions rooted in both scientific evidence (e.g., nitrates cause algal blooms) and cultural principles (e.g., whakapapa, kaitiakitanga).

resources: Local Kaumātua (e.g., Aunty Hine Swift) for pūrākau and mātauranga Māori regarding Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
LAWA (Land, Air, Water Aotearoa) website's 'Can I Swim Here?' and 'Water Quality' data pages for our region
NIWA's 'Freshwater Fish Spawning Maps' and identification guides for native species
Hanna Instruments HI98129 pHep®5 pH and Temperature Tester or similar portable water testing kits

tasks: Submit the completed Stream Health Check field sheet with both scientific and cultural observations.
Write a 300-word analysis paragraph explaining one correlation found between a scientific measurement and a cultural observation.
Create and present the one-page integrated action plan (Te Mahere Herenga Wai) for the stream.

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science - Students will learn about science as a knowledge system that is used by people and that itself changes over time, in the context of mātauranga Māori.

Nature of Science: Investigating in science - Students will extend their experiences and personal explanations of the natural world through exploration, play, asking questions, and discussing simple models in the context of the local stream.
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes, both natural and human-induced.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interrelationship with other Earth systems, particularly the local watershed.
